adbreak / v1.0 / student project
A note from Isaac

Welcome in.
Have a seat.

I built adbreak because when I was trying to break into advertising, the information I needed was scattered everywhere — agency lists on outdated blogs, glossary terms buried in Reddit threads, portfolio advice that contradicted itself every other article.

This is the resource I wish I had. One place. No fluff. Just the actual database, the real framework, the honest glossary, and now — an AI that writes your cold email so you stop staring at a blank screen at midnight.

It's an experiment. It'll keep getting better. But right now, right here — you've got everything you need to start moving. The database has 4,500+ agencies across 186 US cities. The glossary has the language. The portfolio guide has the framework.

The rest is on you. Go make something worth sending.

— Isaac

adbreak is an independent student experiment created for educational purposes only. It is not affiliated with, endorsed by, or connected to any agency, company, or organization referenced within. All information is provided as-is with no guarantee of accuracy. Not for commercial use.

Thanks for contributing. We'll review it and add it to the database if it checks out. You're helping other students find opportunities they wouldn't otherwise know about.

Community
Student Discord

A community for students breaking into advertising — copywriters, art directors, strategists, account people, media planners. Compare notes, share opportunities, get feedback, find accountability partners.

  • #
    agency-intel — Real talk about agencies, cultures, and what it's actually like to work there
  • #
    book-reviews — Get your portfolio reviewed by other students and working creatives
  • #
    internship-hunt — Share leads, rejections, wins, and timelines
  • #
    brief-club — Weekly spec briefs to build your book and stay sharp
  • #
    industry-news — Campaigns, moves, and what's happening in the industry
  • #
    ask-anything — No question too basic. Everyone started somewhere
adbreak is a student project. The Discord is community-run and moderated by students. Be kind. Be helpful. Don't be weird about it.
✓

Invite on its way

Check your inbox. If it doesn't show up in a few minutes, check your spam folder. See you in there.

Community
Mentorship Program

One-to-one connections between students and working advertising professionals. A 30-minute conversation with someone already doing the job you want is worth more than a month of research.

I am a...

Student looking for a mentor → Working professional offering to mentor →
✓

You're in the queue

We'll match you within 1–2 weeks and send an intro email. Since this is a student project, matches are made manually — thanks for your patience.

adbreak is a student project. Mentorship connections are made on a best-effort basis. We cannot guarantee placement or response times. Mentors and students participate voluntarily.
Community
The Brief

The Brief is the adbreak newsletter. No fluff, no sponsored content, no "10 tips to supercharge your career." Just the stuff worth knowing — sent when there's something worth saying.

  • →
    New agencies added to the database that week
  • →
    Campaigns worth studying and why they work
  • →
    Industry moves — who got hired, who left, what's changing
  • →
    Student wins and book feedback from the community
  • →
    One honest piece of career advice per issue

Published roughly twice a month. Unsubscribe anytime. No data sold, no tracking pixels, no nonsense.

✓

You're subscribed

Welcome to The Brief. First issue lands in your inbox when there's something worth saying.

adbreak is a student project. This newsletter is operated on a best-effort basis. Frequency and content may vary.
Community
Contribute to adbreak

adbreak is built and maintained by students, for students. If you want to help make it better, there are a few ways to get involved.

  • ◈
    Agency submissions — Know an agency that's missing? Submit it here.
  • ◈
    Fact-check existing entries — Agency info changes. If you spot something wrong, tell us.
  • ◈
    Write a career piece — First-person accounts of breaking in, intern experiences, industry perspective.
  • ◈
    Mentor a student — A 30-minute conversation makes a real difference. Sign up here.
  • ◈
    Share with your program — The best thing you can do is tell another student about this.
✓

Got it — thank you

Every contribution matters. We'll follow up if we need more from you.

adbreak is a student project. All contributions are voluntary. We reserve the right to edit, decline, or delay submissions at our discretion.

Agency information, including but not limited to size, internship availability, leadership, client rosters, and descriptions, may be inaccurate, incomplete, or out of date. No representation is made as to the accuracy, completeness, or currency of any information contained herein. Users should independently verify all information directly with the relevant agency before making any career, application, or business decisions.

All trademarks, service marks, trade names, and logos referenced on this site are the property of their respective owners. Their inclusion does not imply any affiliation with or endorsement of this project. The AI-generated email content provided by this tool is intended as a starting point only and does not constitute professional career, legal, or business advice. adbreak accepts no responsibility for outcomes resulting from use of content generated by or referenced within this site.

Rate the work. Know the industry.

The Complete Guide

Build a book that
gets you hired.

CDs see 500+ student books a year. Most say the same things, show the same work, solve the same fake briefs. The ones that cut through have one thing in common: they show a point of view, not just execution.

3–5
pieces max
1
clear POV
30s
to hook them
  • 01 The 6-Step Process
  • 02 Case Study Framework
  • 03 Platform Recommendations
  • 04 Do's & Don'ts
  • 05 Launch Checklist

What a strong case study looks like

A real example broken down
Example Case Study
"Dumb Ways to Die" — Metro Trains Melbourne
McCann Melbourne · 2012 · Cannes Grand Prix
Problem

Melbourne's Metro trains had a safety crisis: young people were dying near the tracks doing genuinely dangerous things. Traditional fear-based safety campaigns had low engagement with the exact demographic most at risk.

Insight

You can't scare young people into being safe. But you can make safety culture so entertaining that they adopt it voluntarily. The insight: the best way to make someone care about something serious is to make it genuinely funny first.

The Idea

A catchy animated song about dumb ways to die — with train safety as the punchline, not the premise. Led with entertainment and earned media, using viral distribution to deliver a safety message to an audience that would have ignored a PSA.

Why it works

The brief was about changing behaviour. The insight was counter-intuitive. The execution became a platform — song, game, merch, cultural moment. And it worked: near-miss incidents dropped 21%.

✓ What makes this strong: problem → insight → idea → outcome. Four sentences in order. No adjectives where facts would do.

The 6-Step Portfolio Process

How to go from blank page to booked interview
Step 01

Choose your platform

Your platform choice signals taste. Cargo.site reads as art-directed and intentional. Squarespace is safe and clean. A custom-coded site shows you can. Behance as a primary reads as amateur — use it for visibility only.

Platform
Step 02

Curate ruthlessly: 3–5 pieces max

Your book is only as strong as its weakest piece. CD's read 100+ books a week. Three exceptional pieces beat ten mediocre ones every single time. Cut anything that makes you say "it's not my best work but..."

Curation
Step 03

Write your intro in 2 sentences

State what you make and how you think. No mission statements. No "passionate storyteller." "I'm a copywriter who starts with an uncomfortable truth" is better than "I use words to create emotional connections."

Writing
Step 04

Structure every case study the same way

Consistency helps CDs scan fast. Use the Problem → → Idea → Execution flow. Each section should be 2–3 sentences max. Your actual work should do the heavy lifting — not your description of it.

Structure
Step 05

Show one piece of process per project

Include rough sketches, rejected headlines, or early layouts in at least one project. This shows how you think, not just what you made. Agencies hire thinkers — the work is proof, process is insight.

Process
Step 06

Lock an extended version behind a password

Keep your public book at 3–5 pieces. Maintain an extended version (up to 8 pieces) at /extended with a simple password like brief. Include access instructions in every application email you send.

Strategy

The Case Study Framework

Every great case study follows this structure — in this order
01 — Problem
The Brief
What was the actual challenge? Not the category — the specific tension or problem you were hired to solve.
→
02 — Insight
The Truth
What human truth unlocked the idea? The insight is the hardest part to find and the most important to show.
→
03 — Idea
The Concept
One sentence. If you can't say the idea in one sentence, it's not a clear idea yet. This should make someone nod.
→
04 — Execution
The Work
Show it. Let the work breathe. Don't over-caption. If it needs explaining, the idea wasn't clear enough.
→
05 — Result
The Impact
Awards, press, metrics — whatever is real. For spec work, omit this section. Don't invent results.

Platform Recommendations

Where to host your book and why
◈
Cargo.site
Best-in-class for creative portfolios. The templates signal taste. Widely respected in agencies.
From $13/mo
◻
Squarespace
Clean, professional, reliable. Less distinctive but totally acceptable. Strong template quality.
From $16/mo
⟨/⟩
Custom Coded
If you can build your own site, do it. Shows range. Start with a simple HTML/CSS template.
Domain ~$12/yr
⬜
Behance
Use for discoverability and backup. Never as your primary link in applications. Reads as entry-level.
Free

✓ Do This

Lead with your best piece, always — first impression is everything.
Include one speculative brief if your real work is thin. Label it clearly as spec.
Use a custom domain (yourname.com) — it signals professionalism immediately.
Show work for real or believable brands. Spec for niche brands no one knows reads as lazy briefing.
Have both a PDF version and a live link ready — different CDs ask for different formats.
Update your book every 6 months minimum. Stale work is a red flag.
Send a cover note that's three sentences or fewer. CDs don't read paragraphs.

✗ Avoid This

Don't include work you wouldn't be proud to defend in an interview. If you hesitate, cut it.
Don't lead with your process deck before showing the final work. Hook first, context second.
Don't include more than 8 pieces total, even in your extended version. More isn't better.
Don't use a Behance, Wix, or Weebly URL as your primary link. It signals a student who isn't invested.
Don't write long case study paragraphs. CDs skim. Write for scanners, not readers.
Don't include awards or recognition for student-only competitions in your main stats.
Don't follow up more than once after sending your book. Persistence ≠ enthusiasm in this industry.

Pre-Launch Checklist

Before you send your book to a single agency

adbreak Education

Know the history.
Speak the language.

Agency history, iconic campaigns, 100+ glossary terms, and the people who shaped the industry. Everything you need to walk into any agency and sound like you belong.

Advertising has a 150-year history of ideas that changed culture. Understanding where it came from — and which agencies, people, and philosophies shaped the industry — makes you a better creative and a more credible professional. This is the abridged version. Read it, then go deeper.

Filter:
⌕

The people who built modern advertising. Some of these names are on agency doors. Others wrote the books you should read. All of them left something behind that still shapes how the industry thinks and works.

worth your time.

Award shows to study. Networking orgs with real student access. Portfolio schools ranked by placement. Tools, publications, and the communities that will still matter in year five.

The festivals you'll reference in every portfolio review and every job interview. Learn to talk about these before you need to.

Know where to look.

Career Paths

Where do you
want to go?

Advertising has more entry points than most students realize — and the path isn't always what the portfolio schools advertise. Understand the tracks, the real titles, and what they actually pay. Then choose your angle.

6
Career Tracks
$42K
Entry Floor
$300K+
CD Ceiling
Fast facts
Creatives bill by the idea. Strategists bill by the deck. Account teams bill by the relationship.
Most entry-level roles last 18–24 months before first promotion. Expect to hustle the whole time.
TipPick a track before you graduate. Generalists get passed over at interview stage.
Entry-level: $42K–$68K depending on role and market Mid-level: $70K–$110K Senior: $120K–$180K+ CD-level: $180K–$300K+ Freelance: $500–$2,500/day senior rate

Entry-Level Salary Ranges

US major markets · entry-level ranges

Know your path.

Interview Prep

Interview prep
that actually works.

The questions get asked at every agency. The ones who get hired are the ones who've thought about them before the room — not in the Uber on the way there.

Portfolio Review
They look at your book for 30 seconds. Then they ask you to explain it for 30 minutes.
Creative Brief Test
Most agencies send a brief before the 2nd round. You have 48–72 hours. Make something real.
3
Rounds is standard for creative roles. Portfolio review → brief test → culture fit
24h
Send your thank-you note. Not 48. Not a week. Same day or next morning.
30s
A CD's first glance at your book. The hook has to hit instantly — or they move on.
80%
Of interview rejections are avoidable. They come from not researching the agency beforehand.

Creative Studio

Five tools. One place.
Everything you need to make.

Cold emails, creative briefs, LinkedIn headlines, LinkedIn posts, connection requests — all AI-powered, all built for advertising students. Not templates. Not filler. Things worth sending.

01 Cold Email Generator
02 Creative Brief Generator
03 LinkedIn Headline Builder
04 LinkedIn Post Writer
05 Connection Request Writer
✎
Studio — Coming Soon
AI-powered tools for cold emails, creative briefs, LinkedIn headlines, posts, and connection requests. Requires Claude API integration.
Coming Soon — Requires API Integration
Cold Email Generator
Write the email that actually gets read
Tips
Name a specific piece of work you admire — it's the single biggest differentiator between a real email and a template.
Your "strongest angle" should be a POV, not a credential. "I'm a senior" tells them nothing.
Send Tuesday–Thursday, 9–11am in the recipient's timezone. Not Friday. Never Friday.
✉

Your email will appear here.

Fill in your details and generate.
Quick start:
Creative Brief Generator
Write a brief worth responding to
◈

Your brief will appear here.

Fill in the brand and challenge, or load an example above.
LinkedIn Headline Builder
The most-seen line in your professional life
What makes a great ad industry headline
Lead with your discipline, not your school. "Copywriter" before "VCU Student."
Avoid "passionate storyteller," "creative thinker," "aspiring." Every student on LinkedIn says this.
Your headline appears in search results, connection requests, and comment threads. It should do work on its own, without a profile behind it.
LinkedIn allows 220 characters. Most people use fewer than 60. Use the space.
Lⁿ

Your headlines will appear here.

We'll generate 5 options across different styles. Pick and remix.
LinkedIn Post Writer
Post from your POV, not a template
LinkedIn post strategy for ad students
Write from your discipline's POV. A strategist's take on a campaign should feel different from a copywriter's. Your track is your lens.
Avoid listicles. "5 things I learned..." is the lowest-engagement format and signals student thinking, not professional thinking.
The first line is everything. LinkedIn cuts posts off after ~2 lines. Make the first sentence earnable.
Post once a week minimum. Consistency beats quality in the algorithm and in perception.
✎

Your post will appear here.

Give us specific material — the more detail, the better the post.
Connection Request Writer
300 characters. Make them count.
Connection request rules
Never use the default "I'd like to add you to my LinkedIn network." It says you didn't think about this at all.
Reference something specific — a campaign, a post, a piece of work. It proves you've done homework and filters out the mass-connectors.
Don't pitch in the connection request. Connect first. Ask for things later, if at all.
CDs get dozens of connection requests from students. The ones who get through are the ones who clearly know the work.
◎

Your connection requests will appear here.

We'll write 3 versions — pick the one that sounds most like you.

adbreak Curriculum

Everything you need to know
about advertising. Actually.

Eight modules. Forty lessons. No textbook required. This is the real curriculum — how to think about ideas, how the industry works, how to write the brief before you write the ad. A Gen Z Hey Whipple for the platforms that actually matter.

The complete advertising curriculum

From what advertising actually does to how to survive your first year — this is the course that should exist at every ad school. Work through it in order, or jump to whatever you need right now.

Agency Sentiment Data

Aggregated Scores
Loading sentiment data
Data Export
Export sentiment data as CSV for distribution to agency clients or for analysis.
Export Options
About the data

All exported data is aggregate-only. Individual submissions are never exported with any user-identifying information. The agency_sentiment, tool_reviews, and student_expectations tables do not contain user IDs by design. The trend_votes table contains user IDs for deduplication logic only — it is intentionally excluded from all exports and never shown in this dashboard.

Exported CSVs contain: agency name, city, average creative score, average culture score, average junior-friendly score, average prestige score, overall average, total submission count, and last updated timestamp.

This data is provided for research and business development purposes. adbreak makes no warranty as to the accuracy or representativeness of student perception data.

User Management
View registered users. Promote users to admin by updating their role in the database.
